agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle-class enforcement, and configurable stop duration. This feature spans multiple bounded contexts: Route Planning, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Lifecycle, and Customer Notification.",
    "bounded_context": "Multiple contexts identified: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, stop time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle classes, zone assignments, shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle, pending status, failure handling, zone membership; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification rules, delivery proximity windows, customer preferences.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops computed to minimize total travel time/distance for a driver within their assigned zone during a single shift." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic area that groups deliveries and constrains which drivers can be assigned to them. A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift." },
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver. Route assignment occurs at shift start. A shift is the lifecycle boundary for route planning." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has not yet been attempted, completed, or cancelled — eligible for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that was attempted but could not be completed (e.g., recipient absent, access denied). Triggers route recalculation to remove it from the active route." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of the vehicle a driver operates (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries are physically and legally eligible for that driver." },
      { "term": "Delivery Vehicle Class Requirement", "definition": "A property of a delivery (derived from package dimensions, weight, or contents) that specifies the minimum vehicle class capable of transporting it." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A proximity trigger: when a delivery enters the driver's next 3 planned stops, the customer associated with that delivery receives a notification." },
      { "term": "Route Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the suggested optimized route beyond a defined threshold." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration Estimate", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces recomputation. Three defined triggers: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked failed, driver route deviation." },
      { "term": "Operations Team", "definition": "Internal stakeholders with authority to configure zone-level operational parameters such as stop duration overrides." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery whose vehicle class requirement exceeds the driver's licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Violating this is a physical impossibility (a motorcycle cannot carry freight) and likely a legal/safety violation. This is a hard constraint that, if broken, produces a corrupt assignment state.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D assigned to driver V, D.requiredVehicleClass <= V.licensedVehicleClass."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver MUST only be assigned deliveries within their assigned zone during a shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone assignment is the fundamental partitioning mechanism. Cross-zone assignments would corrupt route optimization and create operational conflicts between drivers.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D assigned to driver V in shift S, D.zone == V.assignedZone(S)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system automatically generates an optimized route from all pending deliveries in their zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The trigger timing (shift start) and automation are policy decisions. A future policy could allow manual route creation or delayed optimization.",
      "testable_assertion": "Within T seconds of a driver's shift starting, an optimized route exists containing all pending deliveries in the driver's zone that match their vehicle class."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's active zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Recalculation policy could be changed to batch updates or time-windowed recalculation to reduce computational cost. This is an operational policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a new delivery is added to zone Z and a driver in Z has an active route, a recalculation is triggered and the new delivery appears in the updated route."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Failed delivery handling is policy. An alternative policy might leave the route unchanged and just skip the stop.",
      "testable_assertion": "When delivery D on driver V's active route is marked failed, a recalculation occurs and D is removed from the active route."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Deviation-triggered recalculation is a UX/operational policy. Could be changed to advisory-only or delayed recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver V's actual position or stop sequence deviates from the planned route beyond the configured threshold, a recalculation is triggered using V's current position as the new origin."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Customers are notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The notification window size (3 stops) and the notification itself are configurable business policies.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a route is calculated or recalculated and delivery D moves into positions 1-3, the customer for D receives a proximity notification exactly once per window entry."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Explicitly described as overridable by operations team. Pure configuration.",
      "testable_assertion": "When no zone-level override exists, the route optimizer uses 4 minutes as the stop duration for ETA calculations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "The operations team can override the stop duration estimate on a per-zone basis.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Administrative configuration capability, not a system invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "When ops sets zone Z stop duration to X minutes, all subsequent route calculations for Z use X instead of the 4-minute default."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 10 pending deliveries in their zone, 2 of which require a vehicle class above the driver's",
      "when": "The system generates the optimized route",
      "then": "The route contains exactly 8 deliveries (the 2 incompatible ones are excluded), ordered for optimal travel, and ETAs use the zone's stop duration (or 4-min default)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active optimized route with 6 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to the driver's zone that matches their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, re-optimized from the driver's current position, and customer notifications are re-evaluated for the new next-3 window"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and stop #4 is marked as a failed delivery",
      "when": "The failure event is processed",
      "then": "The route is recalculated excluding the failed delivery, and if any new deliveries entered the next-3 window as a result, those customers are notified"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and manually skips stop #2 to go to stop #4",
      "when": "The system detects the route deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position, incorporating all remaining undelivered stops (including skipped #2 unless marked failed)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver is on their route and a delivery moves from position #5 to position #3 after a recalculation",
      "when": "The recalculated route is published",
      "then": "The customer for that delivery receives a 'next 3 stops' notification, and customers already notified in a previous window are not re-notified"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A new delivery is added to a zone but it requires a freight vehicle and the only active driver has a motorcycle",
      "when": "Route recalculation is triggered",
      "then": "The delivery is NOT added to the motorcycle driver's route; it remains unassigned pending a compatible driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "The operations team sets the stop duration for Zone A to 6 minutes",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in Zone A",
      "then": "All ETAs in the route use 6 minutes per stop instead of the 4-minute default"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver's shift has not yet started",
      "when": "A new delivery arrives in their zone",
      "then": "No route is generated or recalculated for that driver; the delivery remains in the pending pool"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Multiple drivers active in the same zone simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "The spec does not address how deliveries are partitioned among multiple active drivers in one zone. System must either partition deliveries across drivers (load balancing) or define zone-to-driver as 1:1 per shift. This is a critical ambiguity." },
    { "scenario": "A recalculation is triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress", "expected_behavior": "The system must either queue recalculation requests or cancel-and-restart. Concurrent recalculations for the same route must not produce conflicting route states. Latest-wins semantics recommended." },
    { "scenario": "A new delivery is added to the zone but the driver is on their last stop", "expected_behavior": "Recalculation still occurs — the new delivery becomes the only remaining stop after the current one. If the driver has already completed all stops, the delivery should not be force-assigned but should await the next shift or driver." },
    { "scenario": "All deliveries in a zone fail", "expected_behavior": "After the last failure, the route becomes empty. The driver's shift remains active but with no pending stops. The system should not crash or enter an invalid state on an empty route." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates repeatedly in rapid succession (GPS jitter or urban canyon)", "expected_behavior": "Deviation detection must include a debounce/threshold mechanism to avoid recalculation storms. The spec says 'manually deviates' which implies intentional deviation — GPS noise must not trigger recalculation." },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification sent, then recalculation moves delivery out of the 3-stop window, then back in", "expected_behavior": "Customer should NOT receive duplicate notifications. Notification state must be tracked per delivery per shift to ensure at-most-once semantics within a window-entry cycle." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added that matches the vehicle class but is extremely far from the current route cluster", "expected_behavior": "The optimizer must still include it (it's in the zone), but the route quality may degrade. This is an optimization concern, not a correctness violation — but ETA accuracy for other customers may suffer." },
    { "scenario": "Stop duration override is set to 0 or a negative value", "expected_behavior": "The system must validate stop duration overrides with a minimum bound (>0). A zero-minute stop is physically impossible." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's shift ends mid-route with remaining deliveries", "expected_behavior": "Undelivered stops must return to the pending pool for the zone. They should be eligible for the next driver's route. Customer notifications for undelivered stops should not imply imminent delivery." },
    { "scenario": "New delivery added to zone has ambiguous or missing vehicle class requirement", "expected_behavior": "System should reject the delivery or default to the highest vehicle class to prevent unsafe assignment. This is a data integrity concern." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries-per-shift, and reduces overtime. Dynamic recalculation captures revenue from mid-shift orders that would otherwise wait for the next cycle. Incorrect vehicle class assignment could lead to failed deliveries, redelivery costs, and potential liability.",
    "operational": "Automated routing reduces dispatcher workload significantly. However, frequent recalculations (especially deviation-triggered) may create computational load. Operations team needs a zone configuration UI. Failed delivery handling must integrate with existing retry/escalation workflows.",
    "user": "Customers benefit from 'next 3 stops' notifications — reduces missed deliveries, improves satisfaction. Drivers benefit from not having to plan routes manually. However, frequent recalculations mid-drive could be disorienting for drivers if the UX is not handled carefully (route changing while driving).",
    "risk": "Vehicle class enforcement is safety-critical — a motorcycle carrying freight is a liability and regulatory risk. Notification accuracy is reputation-critical — notifying a customer and then not delivering erodes trust. Recalculation storms from GPS noise or rapid zone changes could degrade system performance.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary users — receive automated routes, experience recalculations mid-shift. UX must be non-disruptive. Must trust the system to give feasible routes matching their vehicle." },
      { "group": "Customers (recipients)", "impact": "Receive proximity notifications. Expectations are set by the notification — failure to deliver after notification causes outsized dissatisfaction." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains zone-level configuration control. Needs tooling to set stop durations. Must monitor unassigned deliveries (vehicle class mismatches, no active driver)." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Automation reduces manual routing workload but may need override capabilities for edge cases the optimizer cannot handle." },
      { "group": "Platform Engineering", "impact": "Must handle recalculation throughput, notification delivery guarantees, and GPS data quality. Route optimization is computationally expensive at scale." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec has several significant ambiguities: (1) multi-driver zone partitioning is unaddressed, (2) deviation detection threshold is undefined, (3) shift end behavior is unspecified, (4) no mention of what happens to vehicle-class-incompatible deliveries (just excluded silently?), (5) notification idempotency requirements are implied but not stated. The core rules are clear but the operational details needed for correct implementation have gaps.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — unambiguous and safety-critical",
      "Three recalculation triggers are clearly enumerated",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is well-defined",
      "Customer notification trigger (next 3 stops) is clear in the happy path"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "How deliveries are partitioned when multiple drivers are active in the same zone",
      "What constitutes a 'deviation' — GPS threshold, sequence deviation, or both",
      "Shift lifecycle boundaries — what happens at shift end with remaining deliveries",
      "Notification idempotency — re-notification rules on repeated window entry",
      "Whether skipped (deviated-past) stops are treated as still pending or implicitly failed",
      "Interaction between recalculation triggers — what if two fire simultaneously"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Zone-to-driver mapping is 1:1 per active shift (not stated, but optimization assumes a single driver owns all zone deliveries)",
      "Route deviation means intentional sequence change, not GPS noise",
      "Failed delivery is a terminal state for that delivery within this shift — not retried on the same route",
      "Customer notification is at-most-once per window entry per shift",
      "Vehicle class is a strict hierarchy (motorcycle < van < truck < freight) not a capability matrix"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not address how deliveries are distributed when multiple drivers are simultaneously active in the same zone. If each driver gets ALL pending deliveries, routes will conflict. If deliveries are partitioned, the partitioning strategy (geographic clustering, load balancing, etc.) is a critical missing business rule.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Zone-to-driver is strictly 1:1 per shift — only one driver active per zone at a time",
        "B: Deliveries are partitioned across active drivers using geographic sub-clustering",
        "C: Deliveries are load-balanced across drivers regardless of geography"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — geographic sub-clustering preserves route optimization quality while allowing multiple drivers per zone. But this requires product decision."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Route deviation is undefined in measurable terms. 'Manually deviates from the suggested route' could mean: (a) driver takes a different road but heads to the correct next stop, (b) driver skips a stop and goes to a later one, or (c) driver's GPS position diverges beyond X meters from the planned path. Each interpretation produces different recalculation behavior.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Platform Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Deviation = driver completes stops out of the suggested sequence",
        "B: Deviation = GPS position diverges > N meters from planned path for > T seconds",
        "C: Both sequence deviation and geographic deviation are tracked independently"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — sequence deviation is the only operationally meaningful trigger. GPS path deviation (taking a different road to the same stop) should not trigger recalculation as the destination order hasn't changed."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No specification for shift-end behavior. When a driver's shift ends with remaining deliveries on the route, the system must handle: returning deliveries to pending pool, cancelling customer notifications that were sent, and preventing new recalculations. This is a critical lifecycle boundary.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Remaining deliveries silently return to pending pool, no customer communication",
        "B: Customers of undelivered stops are proactively notified of delay",
        "C: Shift cannot end while deliveries remain — driver must complete or explicitly fail each"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — customers who received a 'next 3 stops' notification and then don't receive delivery need an explicit update to maintain trust."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Vehicle class compatibility is referenced but the class hierarchy and matching rules are not defined. Is it a strict linear hierarchy (motorcycle < van < truck) where higher classes can handle lower-class deliveries? Or are there capability dimensions (refrigerated, hazmat, oversized) that form a matrix?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Platform Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Strict linear hierarchy — higher class can always handle lower class deliveries",
        "B: Capability matrix — each vehicle class has a set of capabilities, delivery requires a subset match",
        "C: Exact match only — van deliveries go to van drivers, not truck drivers"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — real logistics operations involve capability dimensions (cold chain, hazmat, size), not just a linear scale. But this significantly increases complexity, so Option A may be acceptable for MVP."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
